title: Spring Security
objectives: Configure authN/authZ for a REST API

sections.markdown: Spring Security is a battle-tested framework that plugs into Spring apps as a chain of servlet filters, handling authentication, authorization, session management, CSRF, CORS and common attacks by default.

rows: Every request passes through security filters

sections.markdown: Modern Spring Security is a lambda-DSL — no more WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter. For stateless JWT APIs, wire up `oauth2ResourceServer().jwt()` and let the built-in JWT filter validate tokens; use `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")` for method-level rules.
Disabling CSRF for a session-based browser app (leaves you open to CSRF attacks). Only disable CSRF for stateless API clients that don't use cookies for auth.

For SPAs/mobile: stateless JWT + Resource Server. Hash passwords with BCrypt. Never log tokens. Use `HttpSecurity.headers()` for security headers (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options). Add rate-limit + brute-force protection at the edge.

flashcards.front: How does Spring Security intercept requests?
flashcards.back: Via a chain of servlet filters (SecurityFilterChain).
Authentication = who you are; Authorization = what you can do.

options: A filter chain in front of the app
quiz.explain: Filters process every request before controllers.
Resource Server validates tokens on each request.
CSRF is only relevant when browsers send cookies automatically.

challenges.text: Secure /admin/** for role ADMIN; leave /public open.
Validate JWTs via Resource Server; map scopes to roles.
Add BCrypt password hashing + UserDetailsService with a JPA-backed user repo.
Implement per-method rules with @PreAuthorize + SpEL touching request attributes.

interviewQuestions: How does Spring Security's filter chain work?
How do you protect a REST API with JWTs?
How do you handle password hashing?
